Specifically, the phrase "data or other information" was replaced with "facts or data" in Rule 26(a)(2)(B).[1](https://www.hinshawlaw.com/en/insights/lawyers-for-the-profession-alert/supreme-court-approves-amendments-to-federal-rule-of-civil-procedure-26-limiting-discovery-of-expert-witnesses), [2](https://www.butlersnow.com/wp-content/uploads/pdfs/attorney_publications/rules-update-changes-to-federal-rule-26.pdf), [3](https://www.duanemorris.com/alerts/FRCP_Rule_26_expert_witness_3735.html)

Prior to the December 1, 2010, amendments, Rule 26(a)(2)(B) required experts who were obligated to submit a written report to disclose "facts or other information considered" in forming their opinions.[2](https://www.butlersnow.com/wp-content/uploads/pdfs/attorney_publications/rules-update-changes-to-federal-rule-26.pdf) This broader language was interpreted by courts to mean that virtually anything "considered, reviewed or generated" by a retained expert was discoverable, including draft reports and communications between attorneys and experts.[2](https://www.butlersnow.com/wp-content/uploads/pdfs/attorney_publications/rules-update-changes-to-federal-rule-26.pdf), [3](https://www.duanemorris.com/alerts/FRCP_Rule_26_expert_witness_3735.html)

The change to "facts or data considered by the witness" was intended to narrow the scope of discoverable information and extend work product protection to draft expert reports and certain communications between attorneys and testifying experts.[1](https://www.hinshawlaw.com/en/insights/lawyers-for-the-profession-alert/supreme-court-approves-amendments-to-federal-rule-of-civil-procedure-26-limiting-discovery-of-expert-witnesses), [2](https://www.butlersnow.com/wp-content/uploads/pdfs/attorney_publications/rules-update-changes-to-federal-rule-26.pdf), [3](https://www.duanemorris.com/alerts/FRCP_Rule_26_expert_witness_3735.html) This amendment aimed to address concerns that the previous broad disclosure requirements inhibited open communication between lawyers and expert witnesses, potentially jeopardizing the quality of expert opinions and increasing discovery costs.[2](https://www.butlersnow.com/wp-content/uploads/pdfs/attorney_publications/rules-update-changes-to-federal-rule-26.pdf), [3](https://www.duanemorris.com/alerts/FRCP_Rule_26_expert_witness_3735.html)

The change to "facts or data considered by the witness" was intended to narrow the scope of discoverable information and extend work product protection to draft expert reports and certain communications between attorneys and testifying experts.[1](https://www.hinshawlaw.com/en/insights/lawyers-for-the-profession-alert/supreme-court-approves-amendments-to-federal-rule-of-civil-procedure-26-limiting-discovery-of-expert-witnesses), [2](https://www.butlersnow.com/wp-content/uploads/pdfs/attorney_publications/rules-update-changes-to-federal-rule-26.pdf), [3](https://www.duanemorris.com/alerts/FRCP_Rule_26_expert_witness_3735.html) This amendment aimed to address concerns that the previous broad disclosure requirements inhibited open communication between lawyers and expert witnesses, potentially jeopardizing the quality of expert opinions and increasing discovery costs.[2](https://www.butlersnow.com/wp-content/uploads/pdfs/attorney_publications/rules-update-changes-to-federal-rule-26.pdf), [3](https://www.duanemorris.com/alerts/FRCP_Rule_26_expert_witness_3735.html)

While other rules related to witnesses, such as Rule 30 (depositions by oral examination) and Rule 32 (using depositions in court proceedings), have also undergone amendments, the most notable change involving a "deleted word" in the context of information considered by witnesses in the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, as documented by sources including those related to the Cornell Law School Legal Information Institute, is found within Rule 26 concerning expert witness disclosures.[1](https://www.hinshawlaw.com/en/insights/lawyers-for-the-profession-alert/supreme-court-approves-amendments-to-federal-rule-of-civil-procedure-26-limiting-discovery-of-expert-witnesses), [2](https://www.butlersnow.com/wp-content/uploads/pdfs/attorney_publications/rules-update-changes-to-federal-rule-26.pdf), [3](https://www.duanemorris.com/alerts/FRCP_Rule_26_expert_witness_3735.html)
